Question Text: Why are protists considered paraphyletic?
Option 1: They include many extinct forms, including lineages that no longer have any living representatives.
Option 2: They include some but not all descendants of their most recent common ancestor.
Option 3: They represent all of the descendants of a single common ancestor.
Option 4: Not all protists have all of the synapomorphies that define the Eukarya, such as a nucleus.

Question Text: The most important primary producers in marine ecosystems are ________.
Amoeboid motion is common in species with cell walls.
Some protists use flagella to swim.
Some protists use cilia to swim, which are shorter and more numerous than flagella.
Amoeboid motion requires ATP and interactions between the proteins actin and myosin.
How can dinoflagellates be harmful to humans?
Option 1: They are transmitted by mosquitoes and cause malaria.
Option 2: They produce toxins that can be absorbed by clams and other shellfish which, when eaten by people, can lead to paralytic shellfish poisoning.
Option 3: They cause amoebic dysentery which leads to severe diarrhea and dehydration.
Option 4: They are transmitted by tsetse flies and cause “sleeping sickness.”

Question Text: Explain the logic behind the claim that the nuclear envelope is a synapomorphy that defines eukaryotes as a monophyletic group.
Option 1: All living eukaryotes have a nuclear envelope, so their common ancestor likely did too; bacteria and archaea lack it, so it likely arose in the common ancestor of eukaryotes.

Question Text: Consider the endosymbiosis theory for the origin of the mitochondrion. How did each endosymbiotic partner benefit from the relationship?
Option 1: Host provided protection and carbon compounds; endosymbiont provided increased ATP from those carbon compounds.

Question Text: Why was finding a close relationship between mitochondrial DNA and bacterial DNA considered particularly strong evidence in favor of the endosymbiosis theory?
Option 1: It confirmed a key prediction of the hypothesis and is not well explained by alternative hypotheses.

Question Text: The text claims that protist evolutionary history can be understood as morphological innovations forming seven distinct lineages that later diversified. Explain how the Alveolata support this claim.
Option 1: All alveolates share alveoli (a unique supportive structure), but they diversified in feeding (ingestive/photosynthetic/parasitic) and movement (cilia/flagella/amoeboid-type).

Question Text: Plasmodium has an apicoplast derived from chloroplasts via secondary endosymbiosis; glyphosate poisons an enzyme located in chloroplasts; biologists are testing glyphosate as an antimalarial. How are these observations connected?
Option 1: If the apicoplast is chloroplast-like, glyphosate may poison the apicoplast and kill Plasmodium; humans lack chloroplasts, so it could be selective if no other harmful effects occur.

Question Text: A friend claims warming will make planktonic algae grow faster, removing CO2 faster and burying carbon in shells, reducing global warming. Comment.
Option 1: Primary producers may grow faster with higher CO2, but they have not grown fast enough to reduce atmospheric CO2; CO2 has increased steadily for decades.

Question Text: Which of the following best describes movement in Physarum?
Option 3: The slime mold moves by amoeboid motion.
Option 4: The slime mold moves by gliding motility.

Question Text: Physarum is a plasmodial slime mold, whereas Dictyostelium is a cellular slime mold. Compare and contrast movement by the migrating slug stage of Dictyostelium to the plasmodial stage of Physarum.
Option 1: Physarum plasmodial stage is one large supercell with thousands of nuclei; Dictyostelium migrating slug is an aggregation of many individual cells. Both can move via amoeboid motion.

Question Text: Researchers compared mean time to reach food on plain agar vs agar coated with extracellular slime (P = 0.012). Is the difference significant? What conclusion can be drawn?
Option 1: Yes—significant (P < 0.05). Slime-coated agar caused a statistically significant increase in time to reach the food (navigation took longer).

Question Text: Propose an experiment to test whether extracellular slime coating changed the speed at which the slime mold moved across the substrate.
Option 1: Run two conditions (plain agar vs slime-coated agar), place food a fixed distance away, record time to reach food (replicate); compare speeds/times between conditions.

Question Text: Develop simple experiments to test whether Physarum prefers (1) brightly lit or dark environments; (2) dry or moist conditions; (3) oats or sugar as a food source.
Option 1: Use choice chambers with two halves: (1) light vs dark, (2) dry vs moist, (3) oats vs sugar on opposite sides; place slime mold in middle, observe preference; replicate trials.

Question Text: Researchers have proposed slime molds could help plan future roadways and railways. Justify this statement.
Option 1: Slime molds tend to find the shortest/most efficient route between food sources, conserving energy; this route-optimization behavior can inform efficient road/rail network layouts.
